title: AusSuper chair slams 'flawed' annual test
full_text:
Don Russell, the chairman of the country's largest pension fund, says the annual performance test for the $4.5 trillion sector is structurally broken and has urged an overhaul of the regime to encourage investments that benefit the broader economy.
The AustralianSuper chairman - one of the superannuation industry's most influential figures who had a key presence in the Labor movement throughout the 1990s and was a Department of Industry secretary in the Gillard government - commended Treasurer Jim Chalmers proposed changes to the assessment made earlier this month, but argued this was adding to a "basically flawed system".
"There is a need to go back and revisit [the test] ... if you fail the test, you get taken out and shot", Russell told the 2026 Financial Review BOSS Director Awards.
"We've got to accept that there was a purpose for the original, and it has delivered on that," he said on Tuesday night.
Introduced by the Morrison government and first conducted in 2021, the test is managed by the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority and grades funds on their investment performance over a 10-year period.
Funds are benchmarked against indices that broadly match their asset allocation. If a fund's returns fall 50 basis points short of the benchmark, then it is required to write to members informing them of the miss.
Fail for two years in a row, and the fund is banned from taking on any new members.
Russell said those consequences were "draconian" and all but forced a fund to merge with a rival if they failed, in the most forceful criticism of the regime yet from a major industry player.
While the penalties may have been necessary in the early years, at a time when several underperforming funds were letting their members down, he said the test had now successfully rooted out poor operators.
In last year's performance test, no default MySuper product failed.
By persisting with a years-old structure, Russell said the test was having a negative effect on investment decisions at super funds by incentivising them to passively track indices, rather than funding newer, more productive businesses because they could risk failing the test.
"That is not in the best interests of members. You want all the funds putting their best ideas forward and because we have $4 trillion of wealth tied up there, if you start pulling back from investing in the most active way, there will be macroeconomic consequences," Russell said.
"The performance test was designed for a different purpose. It was recognised, at the time, the longer the performance test in that form stayed around, the greater the possibility that it would have a deleterious effect on how the overall portfolios of funds were invested."
The comments come a little over a week after Chalmers presented a series of options to adjust the test and make it easier for super funds to invest in start-ups, affordable housing, renewable energy products or other projects in the national interest. His consultation paper also tried to address index-hugging concerns as well as disincentives from taking suitable risks.
Treasury's centrepiece proposal was to introduce a new "emerging assets" pool to the test, which would carve out and assess investment in certain areas separately from the returns of more mainstream assets using an inflation-adjusted standard.
Referred to as the "CPI+X" in the paper, big super funds and industry bodies have broadly welcomed the idea.
Russell said on Wednesday it would help alleviate some of his concerns with the regime, but experts and consultants have been more sceptical.
David Bell, executive director of The Conexus Institute retirement think tank, said the idea "sounds logical but will likely prove difficult in practice". "The CPI reference leaves some assets, such as nominal debt and assets in the development phase, exposed to significant performance test risk," Bell said.
Frontier Advisors principal consultant David Carruthers said that there was more merit in exploring Treasury's other proposal to replace the existing asset allocation testing model with simpler reference portfolios.
Rather than assessing funds on their specific strategic allocation, this would require APRA to develop a series of reference portfolios that can be applied to and measured against multiple funds.
Carruthers said this would better account for risk appetite across funds and give a clearer result for members.
"Our position all along with this test is that it needs to take a risk-adjusted approach," he said, noting that funds often invest in assets to diversify their risk, rather than simply for high returns.
"If the test doesn't recognise that risk reduction, then it is not aligning with how super funds invest," he said.
Carruthers argued it was imperative that funds invest in the best long-term interests of their members, rather than be guided by a complex yearly test.
"The current test is making it harder for funds to invest in certain asset classes with the benchmarks as they are defined. All we are saying to the government is: take away the brake that you didn't mean to apply," he said.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Lucas Baird
heading: Superannuation and Investments
